name: hive-mind-advanced
description: Advanced Hive Mind collective intelligence system for queen-led multi-agent coordination with consensus mechanisms and persistent memory
capabilities: Spawns and coordinates multiple worker agents under queen-led supervision for parallel task execution
Implements consensus voting mechanisms for collective decision-making across agent swarms
Maintains persistent shared memory for cross-agent context and learnings
Manages task delegation with dynamic load balancing based on worker capabilities
Enables emergent problem-solving through agent-to-agent communication protocols
Tracks collective progress and synthesizes results from distributed agent outputs
author: Claude Flow Team
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 108 potential issues, all evaluated as false positives. Network and URL findings are legitimate source URL references. Cryptographic algorithm alerts are pattern matching errors triggered by text substrings. External command findings are documentation examples, not code vulnerabilities. This is a legitimate multi-agent coordination system.
valueStatement: Complex problems require coordinated effort from multiple AI agents. Hive Mind provides a queen-led architecture that enables consensus-based decision-making and shared memory for collective problem-solving across distributed agent swarms.
userTitle: Coordinate Multi-Agent AI Teams with Hive Mind
limitations: Requires careful queen prompt design to avoid conflicting worker instructions
Shared memory size constraints may limit context retention for very long tasks
Consensus voting adds latency for time-critical single-agent decisions
Complex swarm topologies may become difficult to debug and monitor

useCases.targetUser: Security engineers and architects reviewing complex codebases
useCases.title: Parallel Codebase Analysis
useCases.description: Deploy multiple agents to analyze different subsystems of a large codebase simultaneously. The queen agent synthesizes findings into a comprehensive security audit or architecture review.

useCases.targetUser: Technical writers and content teams producing complex documentation
useCases.title: Collaborative Writing Projects
useCases.description: Coordinate agents with different expertise to research, outline, draft, and review documentation or technical content in parallel, ensuring consistency through shared memory.

useCases.targetUser: Researchers and analysts synthesizing information from diverse sources
useCases.title: Research Synthesis
useCases.description: Task multiple agents with exploring different research topics or data sources. The hive mind aggregates insights and identifies patterns across all collected information.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Hive Mind Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Activate Hive Mind with 3 worker agents. Queen directive: [describe overall goal]. Workers should coordinate to [specific task]. Report findings back to queen for synthesis.
promptTemplates.scenario: Getting started with simple parallel task execution

promptTemplates.title: Consensus-Based Decision
promptTemplates.prompt: Deploy 5 workers to analyze [problem] from different angles. Each worker should propose a solution approach. Use consensus voting to select the best path forward. Queen to break ties.
promptTemplates.scenario: Making collective decisions with multiple perspectives

promptTemplates.title: Research Swarm
promptTemplates.prompt: Queen directive: Research [topic]. Workers specialize: one searches documentation, one analyzes examples, one tests hypotheses, one reviews limitations. Share findings in shared memory. Synthesize comprehensive report.
promptTemplates.scenario: Coordinated research with specialized agent roles

promptTemplates.title: Iterative Refinement Loop
promptTemplates.prompt: Initialize Hive Mind for iterative improvement. Queen assigns workers to: generate solution, critique solution, improve solution, validate solution. Loop until consensus on quality threshold.
promptTemplates.scenario: Quality assurance and iterative improvement workflows

bestPractices: Define clear queen directives that establish goals without over-constraining worker autonomy
Use specialized worker roles for distinct concerns to avoid redundant work
Set explicit consensus thresholds and tie-breaking rules for decision-making
Monitor shared memory for conflicting conclusions and resolve contradictions
antiPatterns: Overloading the queen with micro-management that defeats parallel execution benefits
Deploying workers with overlapping responsibilities causing redundant effort
Ignoring consensus minority views that may represent important edge cases
Failing to establish memory cleanup policies for long-running swarms

faq.question: What is the difference between Hive Mind and running multiple independent agents?
faq.answer: Hive Mind provides structured coordination through queen-led supervision, consensus voting, and shared memory. Independent agents lack these coordination mechanisms, leading to fragmented outputs and duplicated effort.

faq.question: How many workers can I deploy in a single hive?
faq.answer: The optimal number depends on task complexity and available context. Start with 3-5 workers and scale based on the parallelism your task can sustain without overwhelming coordination overhead.

faq.question: What happens when workers reach conflicting conclusions?
faq.answer: The consensus mechanism allows workers to vote on conclusions. The queen can break ties, request additional analysis, or synthesize partial agreement into a refined conclusion.

faq.question: How is shared memory managed across worker lifecycles?
faq.answer: Shared memory persists for the duration of the hive session. The queen manages memory cleanup and ensures workers access relevant context without interference from stale data.

faq.question: Can Hive Mind agents communicate with each other directly?
faq.answer: Workers primarily communicate through the queen and shared memory. This design prevents message chaos and ensures the queen maintains oversight of all inter-agent coordination.

faq.question: What types of tasks work best with Hive Mind?
faq.answer: Tasks that decompose naturally into parallel subtasks with distinct expertise requirements benefit most. Complex analysis, research synthesis, and comprehensive reviews are ideal use cases.
